summary: Gruber uses Google's five-year delay in adding split-screen multitasking to the iPad Gmail app as a springboard to argue that Apple's App Store review process has its priorities backwards. Rather than focusing on extracting maximum revenue from developers, Apple should use its monopoly power over iOS app distribution to enforce quality standards and platform citizenship. He contends that the App Store was originally conceived to foster excellent software, not generate services revenue, and that allowing the most popular productivity app on iPad to languish without basic platform features is as damning as the Hey controversy. Gruber envisions an alternative App Store regime where apps are rejected for poor user experience rather than insufficient revenue sharing, arguing this would be a novel and defensible use of market power.
key_points: Google took five years to add split-screen multitasking to iPad Gmail — a foundational platform feature for an email client — revealing how little Google cares about native iOS experience
Apple's App Store review should prioritize enforcing quality and platform citizenship over revenue extraction, especially for the most popular apps
The tolerance for mediocre apps signals a cultural shift: Apple users used to complain vociferously about bad software, and that attitude may be eroding within Apple itself
Steve Jobs explicitly said Apple didn't intend to profit from the App Store — today's revenue-focused approach is irreconcilable with that founding vision
Apple's great power as the sole gatekeeper of iOS software should be wielded in service of user experience, not monetization
money_quotes: It's like the difference between showing up fashionably late and showing up a week after the party.
Google makes a lot of software with terrible user experiences for users who have poor taste.
What exactly is the point of running a strict approval process for apps if not, first and foremost, to ensure that they're good apps?
But what made Apple users Apple users is that they complained vociferously if they had to use a terrible app.

I'd like to see all the vim, vigor, and vigilance Apple applies to making sure no app on the App Store is making a dime without Apple getting three cents applied instead to making sure there aren't any scams or rip-offs.
You can't pack every last ounce of joy, beauty, and elegance into something while simultaneously trying to squeeze every last dollar out of it.
This company is abusing its market dominance to force us to make our apps better for users would be delightful new territory.
Profit seeking, as an end unto itself, does not generate excellence — and in fact generally results in the opposite.
key_insight: Apple's App Store review process should use its monopoly power to enforce software quality and platform citizenship rather than maximize revenue extraction from developers.
